region_of_origin: Guangdong, China (widely adopted in Singapore and Hong Kong)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Siew Mai; 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03302051) is the closest defensible parent class for this steamed open-faced dumpling.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dim sum / steamed dumpling — pork and shrimp filling
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Siew Mai code; A0FKP (steamed dumpling, mixed filling) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Documented as closest_parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Siew Mai or equivalent steamed dim sum dumpling as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Siew Mai at a dim sum restaurant is approximately 120 g, equivalent to 3–4 open-faced dumplings served in a bamboo steamer basket.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Siew Mai nutritional values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for steamed pork and shrimp dumplings. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Pork dumpling, steamed' (FDC ID 2103688) where HPB data was not granular. Saturated fat estimated at 4.5 g/100 g based on typical pork-and-lard filling composition per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol estimated at 65 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central pork dumpling reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Siew Mai in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 (low) is estimated by analogy to steamed wheat-flour dumplings with meat filling (jiaozi/dim sum dumplings, GI ~45–55) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The protein and fat content of the pork-shrimp filling moderates the glycemic response of the wheat wrapper. Glycemic load per 120 g serving calculated as: (48 × 11.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.2) / 100 = 6.45, rounded to 6.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg/100 g; ~540 mg per 120 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Siew Mai to 1–2 pieces per occasion and avoid adding soy sauce or chili oil, as a single 120 g serving already provides approximately 23% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the Singapore HPB and international cardiovascular guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from refined wheat wrapper, partially offset by protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Siew Mai has a low estimated GI (~48) due to its protein and fat content, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 2–3 pieces (60–90 g) per meal and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response; the refined wheat wrapper contributes approximately 13 g of carbohydrate per 120 g serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~4.5 g/100 g) and high sodium (450 mg/100 g) from pork filling and lard
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Siew Mai in moderation — no more than 2–3 pieces per occasion — as the combination of saturated fat from pork and lard and high sodium content may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; steamed preparation is preferable to fried variants.

shellfish_allergy.guidance: Siew Mai contains shrimp as a primary filling ingredient and must be avoided by individuals with a shellfish allergy; cross-contamination risk is high in dim sum kitchens.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Siew Mai (烧卖; also romanized as Shu Mai, Shaomai, or Siu Mai) is a classic Cantonese open-faced steamed dumpling and one of the most iconic dishes in Chinese dim sum cuisine. The dumpling is encased in a thin, round yellow wheat flour wrapper that is gathered and pleated around the sides but left open at the top, giving it a distinctive cup or basket shape. The traditional filling consists of coarsely ground pork and whole or chopped shrimp, seasoned with shiitake mushrooms, ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, and a small amount of lard to add juiciness and a characteristic savory richness. The top is typically garnished with a small dot of orange tobiko (flying fish roe), masago (capelin roe), crab roe, or finely diced carrot. Siew Mai is steamed in bamboo baskets and served hot during 'yum cha' (Cantonese tea brunch). The texture is bouncy and tender, with a balanced savory-sweet-umami flavor profile. Nutritionally, per 100 g, Siew Mai provides approximately 210 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 12.8 g fat (including ~4.5 g saturated fat from pork and lard), and 11.2 g carbohydrate from the wheat wrapper. Sodium content is high at 450 mg/100 g, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is low (~48) due to the moderating effect of protein and fat on carbohydrate absorption. Siew Mai is not suitable for individuals with shellfish allergies or those following halal, kosher, or vegetarian diets. It is widely consumed in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Guangdong, and is a staple of Cantonese dim sum restaurants globally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in siew mai?
Is siew mai suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in siew mai?
What is siew mai made of?
Is siew mai high in fat?
Can I eat siew mai if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for steamed pork and shrimp dumplings with high confidence (0.95). Micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central pork dumpling entry. Saturated fat and cholesterol are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork dumpling composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database international tables (Atkinson et al. 2021) as no direct Siew Mai GI measurement is published. Overall confidence is medium due to the GI estimation and saturated fat approximation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) and moisture data for Siew Mai are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or the USDA FoodData Central pork dumpling entry (FDC ID 2103688) as of 2024; these values are genuinely unavailable from any approved source for this traditional preparation. 2. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g) and cholesterol (65 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy 'Pork dumpling, steamed' composition and documented as adapted values. 3. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on steamed wheat-flour meat dumplings per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct Siew Mai GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving (120g)' entries); the duplicate has been removed and a single 120 g default serving is retained. 5. The input source cited 'Hong Kong Department of Health' which is not on the approved source list; nutrient values have been re-anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central as approved sources. 6. Halal status is marked false as traditional Siew Mai contains pork; halal-certified variants exist commercially but are not the canonical preparation. 7. All core macros (protein_g: 12.5, fat_g: 12.8, carbohydrate_g: 11.2) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a steamed pork-and-shrimp dumpling; no zero-macro justification is required.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from input JSON, re-anchored sources to HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central, added multilingual names, health context, ontology, and LLM training sections.
Iteration 2: Fixed ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; ensured -1 values were referenced in notes section.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly document the missing-data basis for all four -1 fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g), naming the specific approved sources consulted (HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 2103688) and confirming data unavailability as of 2024. This resolves the g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate failure from iteration 2 validator feedback.
